reasoning:
The market resolves to "Yes" if the BLS reports a 12-month CPI increase of 4.1% or more for any month in 2025. Data released up through September 2025 shows year-over-year CPI at or below 3.0% for all months (Jan: 3.0%, Feb: 2.8%, Mar: 2.4%, Apr: 2.3%, May: 2.4%, Jun: 2.7%, Jul: 2.7%, Aug: 2.9%, Sep: 3.0%).

With only October, November, and December 2025 reports remaining, the YoY CPI would need to jump from 3.0% to at least 4.1% (a 1.1 percentage point increase). Assuming trailing months from Q4 2024 average ~0.2%-0.3%, the monthly CPI increases for the rest of 2025 would need to average near 0.6% or more to hit 4.1%. Major institutional forecasts (like J.P. Morgan) anticipate inflation ending the year around 3.5% at worst. While some tariffs are currently exerting upward pressure, reaching an annualized run rate necessary to bump the YoY figure to 4.1% inside just three months would require a massive, immediate macroeconomic shock (such as a severe energy crisis), which is not currently present. 

Thus, the likelihood of inflation reaching the 4.1% threshold in 2025 is extremely low.
